title: Resolving spurious topological entanglement entropy in stabilizer codes
authors: Peilun Han, Zijian Liang, Yifei Wang, Bowen Yang, Yingfei Gu, Yu-An Chen
abstract: Topological entanglement entropy (TEE) is a key diagnostic of long-range entanglement in two-dimensional gapped phases of matter, but it can suffer from spurious contributions that overestimate the total quantum dimension of the underlying topological order. In this work, we identify the microscopic origin of spurious TEE and introduce a concave partition for computing the Levin-Wen TEE of translation-invariant stabilizer codes of prime-dimensional qudits. We rigorously prove that this prescription is free of spurious contributions. As a complementary probe, we study bivariate bicycle codes on a bipartite cylinder and show that the entanglement entropy depends sensitively on the cylinder circumference, revealing topological frustration of the underlying anyons.
summary: This paper resolves the problem of artificial entropy contributions in the calculation of topological entanglement entropy for stabilizer codes. By replacing standard rectangular partitions with a specifically designed concave geometry, the authors prove that the genuine topological order can be accurately recovered. The work also demonstrates how entanglement entropy can reveal topological frustration in quantum LDPC codes.
main_problem: The paper addresses the issue of spurious topological entanglement entropy (sTEE) in stabilizer codes, where standard rectangular partitions produce artificial constant contributions that overestimate the total quantum dimension.
main_result: The authors introduce a concave partition geometry that eliminates spurious contributions and provide a rigorous proof that this prescription recovers the genuine topological entanglement entropy for translation-invariant stabilizer codes of prime-dimensional qudits.
method: The study uses algebraic techniques including Gröbner basis reduction, Dubé's theorem for degree bounds, and the Smith Normal Form to analyze stabilizer group sizes and the decomposition of boundary gauge operators.
model_or_system: The work focuses on 2D translation-invariant stabilizer codes of prime-dimensional qudits (Zp), specifically examining models like the toric code, shifted toric code, and bivariate bicycle codes on a cylinder.
key_observables: Topological entanglement entropy (TEE), bipartite entanglement entropy, and total quantum dimension.
important_parameters: Stabilizer range (r), qudit dimension (q), system size (L), and cylinder circumference (l).
main_assumptions: The results are specifically derived for translation-invariant stabilizer codes of prime-dimensional qudits; the derived analytic lower bound for the partition size is noted to be conservative.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares rectangular and concave partitions; Figure 2 and 3 illustrate stabilizer decomposition and the removal of residual support; Figure 4 shows bipartite entanglement entropy on a cylinder; Figure 5 shows the shifted toric code stabilizers; Figure 15 shows a schematic of stabilizer decomposition.
paper_structure: The paper identifies the origin of sTEE, introduces the concave partition, provides a rigorous mathematical proof for the absence of spurious terms using algebraic geometry, and applies the method to study topological frustration in bivariate bicycle codes.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Our main result shows that the concave partition is immu- nized against spurious contributions to the topological entan- glement entropy.

FIG. 4. Bipartite entanglement entropy on an infinite cylin- der. (a) An infinite cylinder is bipartitioned into regions A and B by a cut perpendicular to the cylinder axis. After un- folding, the cut induces a rough boundary on the right edge of region A and a smooth boundary on the left edge of region B. The top and bottom edges are identified, and the cylin- der circumference is l. (b) The entanglement entropy SA(l) of region A for the (3, 3)-BB code [23]. The numerical data separate into four linear branches, SA(l) = 3l, 3l −4, 3l −6, and 3l −8, depending on gcd(l, 12). The constant offset is in one-to-one correspondence with k(l)/2, where k(l) is the dimension of logical operators...

FIG. 5. sTEE of the shifted toric code. (a) The stabilizers of the shifted toric code obtained from the standard toric code by a circuit of CNOT gates. The red and blue Pauli operators denote the Av and Bp terms, respectively, and the corresponding dots mark their lattice positions. (b) In the rectangular partition, the green, orange, and black regions represent regions A, C, and B, respectively. The X-type and Z-type stabilizers, formed by taking products of the Av operators and Bp operators marked by the corresponding dots, contribute to the spurious TEE.

FIG. 6. Left: The geometry of the Levin-Wen prescription. Right: An anyon a is imported inside the annulus by a Pauli string, flipping the expectation value of the Wilson loop b that detects this anyon. See Lemma 6 for more details.
